• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Project3

Alex Jones and Hudson Plaskoff

2026-05-01

Introduction

For this project, we decided to examine potential relationships between the stock prices of companies with close ties to the US housing market and economic indicators based on trends in home construction and sales.

The companies we chose to look at are some of the largest publicly traded firms that are related to the housing market. They are listed below, along with their corresponding industry:1

  • HD - Home Depot (Home Improvement Retail)

  • LOW - Lowe’s (Home Improvement Retail)

  • DHI - D. R. Horton (Residential Construction)

  • PHM - PulteGroup (Residential Construction)

  • LEN - Lennar (Residential Construction)

  • MAS - Masco (Building Products & Equipment)

  • BLDR - Builders Firstsource (Building Products & Equipment)

  • OC - Owens Corning (Building Products & Equipment)

We decided to only look at data from January 1, 2007 and later due to not all of our selected companies being publicly traded for much of the previous year. To do this, we used Python to import stock data for selected companies that are related to the US housing market. We also imported the following datasets from FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data) from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis:

  • Producer Price Index by Commodity: Special Indexes: Construction Materials
    • Measures the relative cost of construction materials
  • Total Construction Spending: Residential in the United States
    • Measures total construction spending (in millions of $) by companies that build residential buildings
  • New Privately-Owned Housing Units Started: Total Units
    • Measures the total number of new homes (in thousands) starting to be constructed
  • New One Family Houses Sold: United States
    • Measures the total number of new one family homes sold (in thousands)

We don’t anticipate that we will use all of these datasets (stocks or economic data), but we believe that it would be beneficial to have them imported and ready to use in case we needed one of them that we hadn’t anticipated using.

Across all of the FRED datasets, the sample size is 228, which in this context of monthly observations means the data is from a 228 month (19 year) period of time. The sample size for the stock data across all of the stocks chosen is also 228, meaning it is also across 228 months (or 19 years) in the same period of time as the FRED data.

Data Import

Stock Data from Python (yfinance)

import yfinance as yf
import pandas as pd

tickers = ["HD","LOW","DHI","PHM","LEN","MAS","BLDR","OC"]
start = "2007-01-01"
end = "2025-12-31"

stock_data = yf.download(tickers,start=start,end=end)
## [                       0%                       ][************          25%                       ]  2 of 8 completed[******************    38%                       ]  3 of 8 completed[**********************50%                       ]  4 of 8 completed[**********************62%*****                  ]  5 of 8 completed[**********************75%***********            ]  6 of 8 completed[**********************88%*****************      ]  7 of 8 completed[*********************100%***********************]  8 of 8 completed
stock_close = stock_data["Close"]
stock_close <- py$stock_close

stock_close <- stock_close %>%
  mutate(
    date = rownames(stock_close)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    date = ymd_hms(date)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
     year = year(date),
     month = month(date),
     mday = day(date)
  )

stock_close_month <- stock_close %>%
  group_by(year,month) %>%
  summarize(
    HD = mean(HD),
    LOW = mean(LOW),
    DHI = mean(DHI),
    PHM = mean(PHM),
    LEN = mean(LEN),
    MAS = mean(MAS),
    BLDR = mean(BLDR),
    OC = mean(OC)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    day = 1,
    .after = month
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year,month,day),
    .before = year
  )
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'year'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
stock_close_month_pivoted <- stock_close_month %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = HD:OC,
    names_to = "stock",
    values_to = "price"
  )

knitr::kable(stock_close_month %>% head())
date year month day HD LOW DHI PHM LEN MAS BLDR OC
2007-01-01 2007 1 1 24.68300 23.37028 21.12306 27.24821 39.72749 18.17778 14.66739 23.12713
2007-02-01 2007 2 1 25.14243 23.91047 21.87977 26.83038 40.71514 18.59314 15.67748 25.38792
2007-03-01 2007 3 1 23.40291 22.54805 18.22042 22.81712 35.43931 16.58338 14.41387 25.49823
2007-04-01 2007 4 1 23.72223 22.24777 17.42128 22.57954 33.01320 16.28417 14.12552 26.66170
2007-05-01 2007 5 1 23.90247 22.41345 17.94737 22.16025 34.34743 17.83118 13.69103 27.73850
2007-06-01 2007 6 1 23.98724 22.47433 16.75247 20.57656 31.93115 17.12879 13.79899 28.60417

We used the yfinance library in Python to easily access stock data from Yahoo Finance for our selected companies in the date range we indicated (January 2007 to December 2025). We then imported the created dataset into R and generated a mean closing price for each month, which was then assigned to be the first of each respective month so it could be more easily joined to datasets from FRED, which also assign the day as one for a given month’s data.

Economic Data from FRED

ppi_construction <- read_csv("WPUSI012011.csv") %>%
  rename(
    ppi_construction = WPUSI012011
  )
## Rows: 228 Columns: 2
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl  (1): WPUSI012011
## date (1): observation_date
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
construction_spending <- read_csv("TLRESCONS.csv") %>%
  rename(
    construction_spending = TLRESCONS
  )
## Rows: 228 Columns: 2
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl  (1): TLRESCONS
## date (1): observation_date
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
new_private_units <- read_csv("HOUST.csv") %>%
  rename(
    new_private_units = HOUST
  )
## Rows: 228 Columns: 2
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl  (1): HOUST
## date (1): observation_date
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
new_family_homes_sold <- read_csv("HSN1F.csv") %>%
  rename(
    new_family_homes_sold = HSN1F
  )
## Rows: 228 Columns: 2
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl  (1): HSN1F
## date (1): observation_date
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
fred_data <- ppi_construction %>%
  left_join(construction_spending) %>%
  left_join(new_private_units) %>%
  left_join(new_family_homes_sold) %>%
  rename(
    date = observation_date
  )
## Joining with `by = join_by(observation_date)`
## Joining with `by = join_by(observation_date)`
## Joining with `by = join_by(observation_date)`
fred_data_pivoted <- fred_data %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = ppi_construction:new_family_homes_sold,
    names_to = "economic_metric",
    values_to = "value"
  )

knitr::kable(fred_data %>% head())
date ppi_construction construction_spending new_private_units new_family_homes_sold
2007-01-01 181.1 544096 1409 891
2007-02-01 181.5 538260 1480 828
2007-03-01 182.5 534655 1495 833
2007-04-01 183.7 530749 1490 887
2007-05-01 184.0 521499 1415 842
2007-06-01 184.0 513524 1448 793

The data import for the FRED data was simpler than the data import for the stocks. This was due largely to the option on the FRED website to download CSVs for each of the economic variables, which we then imported into R. This process was also helped by the date variable in each of these datasets being formatted as date data and also being set to the first of the month for each respective observation.

Exploratory Data Analysis

Stock Prices

ggplot(stock_close_month_pivoted,aes(x=date,y=price,color=stock)) +
  geom_line()

stock_close_month_pivoted_data <- stock_close_month_pivoted %>%
  group_by(stock) %>%
  summarize(
    min_price = min(price),
    mean_price = mean(price),
    median_price = median(price),
    max_price = max(price),
  )

knitr::kable(stock_close_month_pivoted_data)
stock min_price mean_price median_price max_price
BLDR 1.101346 35.42892 13.07095 202.51750
DHI 5.002928 47.31479 27.35712 186.80038
HD 13.606933 142.77021 102.92211 407.72163
LEN 5.327908 51.88619 40.42430 172.65447
LOW 11.931999 90.05258 61.28343 263.69779
MAS 3.764498 30.69839 27.28967 80.79327
OC 6.531154 56.58260 40.38724 188.70071
PHM 3.643579 33.58909 19.44434 136.73100

Economic Data

PPI (Construction Materials)

ggplot(fred_data,aes(x=date,y=ppi_construction)) +
  geom_line()

ppi_construction_data <- fred_data %>%
  summarize(
    min_ppi = min(ppi_construction),
    mean_ppi = mean(ppi_construction),
    median_ppi = median(ppi_construction),
    max_ppi = max(ppi_construction),
  )

knitr::kable(ppi_construction_data)
min_ppi mean_ppi median_ppi max_ppi
181.1 241.9401 215.5 353.015

Residential Construction Spending

construction_spending_data <- fred_data %>%
  summarize(
    min_spending = min(construction_spending),
    mean_spending = mean(construction_spending),
    median_spending = median(construction_spending),
    max_spending = max(construction_spending),
  )

min_spending mean_spending median_spending max_spending

new_family_homes_sold_data <- fred_data %>%
  summarize(
    min_new_sold = min(new_family_homes_sold),
    mean_new_sold = mean(new_family_homes_sold),
    median_new_sold = median(new_family_homes_sold),
    max_new_sold = max(new_family_homes_sold),

Research

Research Question

To what extent does there exist a correlation between various economic metrics and publicly traded equities related to the US housing market?

joined_stock_economic_data <- fred_data %>%
  left_join(stock_close_month) %>%
  select(
    !(year:day)
  )
## Joining with `by = join_by(date)`
knitr::kable(joined_stock_economic_data %>% head())
date ppi_construction construction_spending new_private_units new_family_homes_sold HD LOW DHI PHM LEN MAS BLDR OC
2007-01-01 181.1 544096 1409 891 24.68300 23.37028 21.12306 27.24821 39.72749 18.17778 14.66739 23.12713
2007-02-01 181.5 538260 1480 828 25.14243 23.91047 21.87977 26.83038 40.71514 18.59314 15.67748 25.38792
2007-03-01 182.5 534655 1495 833 23.40291 22.54805 18.22042 22.81712 35.43931 16.58338 14.41387 25.49823
2007-04-01 183.7 530749 1490 887 23.72223 22.24777 17.42128 22.57954 33.01320 16.28417 14.12552 26.66170
2007-05-01 184.0 521499 1415 842 23.90247 22.41345 17.94737 22.16025 34.34743 17.83118 13.69103 27.73850
2007-06-01 184.0 513524 1448 793 23.98724 22.47433 16.75247 20.57656 31.93115 17.12879 13.79899 28.60417 
joined_stock_economic_data_pivoted <- joined_stock_economic_data %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = ppi_construction:OC,
    names_to = "metric",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    type = case_when(
      metric == "ppi_construction" | metric == "construction_spending" | metric == "new_private_units" | metric == "new_family_homes_sold" ~ "economy",
      .default = "stock"
    )
  )

knitr::kable(joined_stock_economic_data_pivoted %>% head())
date metric value type
2007-01-01 ppi_construction 181.10000 economy
2007-01-01 construction_spending 544096.00000 economy
2007-01-01 new_private_units 1409.00000 economy
2007-01-01 new_family_homes_sold 891.00000 economy
2007-01-01 HD 24.68300 stock
2007-01-01 LOW 23.37028 stock

D. R. Horton and Producer Price Index of Construction Materials

coeff <- 2

ggplot(joined_stock_economic_data,aes(x=date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y=DHI),color="orange") + 
  geom_line(aes(y=ppi_construction/coeff),color="blue") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    # First Axis
    name = "D. R. Horton stock price",
    # Second Axis
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~.*coeff, name="PPI Construction")
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2020-03-11")), color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  annotate("rect", 
           xmin = as.Date("2020-03-16"), 
           xmax = as.Date("2022-03-16"), 
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, 
           alpha = 0.2, fill = "yellow") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2016-01-01"), y=175, label= "COVID pandemic declared") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2023-05-01"), y=35, label= "Rates held near zero")
## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

dhr_by_ppi <- joined_stock_economic_data %>%
  summarize(
    correlation = cor(DHI,ppi_construction)
  )

knitr::kable(dhr_by_ppi)
correlation
0.9111846

Masco and Residential Construction Spending

coeff3 <- 10000

ggplot(joined_stock_economic_data,aes(x=date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y=MAS),color="orange") + 
  geom_line(aes(y=construction_spending/coeff3),color="blue") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    # First Axis
    name = "Masco stock price",
    # Second Axis
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~.*coeff3, name="Construction Spending")
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2020-03-11")), color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  annotate("rect", 
           xmin = as.Date("2020-03-16"), 
           xmax = as.Date("2022-03-16"), 
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, 
           alpha = 0.2, fill = "yellow") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2016-01-01"), y=100, label= "COVID pandemic declared") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2023-05-01"), y=10, label= "Rates held near zero")

mas_by_construction <- joined_stock_economic_data %>%
  summarize(
    correlation = cor(MAS,construction_spending)
  )

knitr::kable(mas_by_construction)
correlation
0.9561327

Home Depot and New Private Housing Units Started

coeff2 <- 10

ggplot(joined_stock_economic_data,aes(x=date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y=HD),color="orange") + 
  geom_line(aes(y=new_private_units/coeff2),color="blue") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    # First Axis
    name = "Home Depot stock price",
    # Second Axis
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~.*coeff2, name="New Private Units")
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2020-03-11")), color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  annotate("rect", 
           xmin = as.Date("2020-03-16"), 
           xmax = as.Date("2022-03-16"), 
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, 
           alpha = 0.2, fill = "yellow") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2016-01-01"), y=400, label= "COVID pandemic declared") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2023-05-01"), y=70, label= "Rates held near zero")

hd_by_new_units <- joined_stock_economic_data %>%
  summarize(
    correlation = cor(HD,new_private_units)
  )

knitr::kable(hd_by_new_units)
correlation
0.7591778

Lowe’s and New One Family Homes Sold

coeff2 <- 5

ggplot(joined_stock_economic_data,aes(x=date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y=LOW),color="orange") + 
  geom_line(aes(y=new_family_homes_sold/coeff2),color="blue") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    # First Axis
    name = "Lowe's stock price",
    # Second Axis
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~.*coeff2, name="New Family Homes Sold")
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2020-03-11")), color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  annotate("rect", 
           xmin = as.Date("2020-03-16"), 
           xmax = as.Date("2022-03-16"), 
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, 
           alpha = 0.2, fill = "yellow") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2016-01-01"), y=400, label= "COVID pandemic declared") +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2023-05-01"), y=70, label= "Rates held near zero")

hd_by_homes_sold <- joined_stock_economic_data %>%
  summarize(
    correlation = cor(BLDR,new_family_homes_sold)
  )

knitr::kable(hd_by_homes_sold)
correlation
0.4566098
